voice: Warm, sensory, and unhurried — a neighborhood baker who talks about flour, fire, and the four o'clock hour like they matter, because they do.

designTokens.paletteMood: Stone-ground cream, terracotta crust, and deep cocoa crumb — the exact colors of a loaf pulled at dawn.

title.value: Bread made the slow way, on Alder Street

subtitle.value: Ember & Rye is a wood-fired neighborhood bakery in the Bishop's Mill quarter of Portland, turning out naturally leavened sourdough, laminated pastry, and a rotating chalkboard of morning bakes seven days a week. Everything is mixed by hand and baked before you wake.

data.title: Rosa started with one oven and a stubborn starter named Nub
data.description: Rosa Bellani learned to bake at her grandmother's shoulder in Sétif, where the bread was flat, blistered, and never sliced with a knife. She opened Ember & Rye in 2016 in a former print shop on Alder Street, keeping the terrazzo floors and adding a single brick-lined deck oven she fires with oak offcuts from a mill two blocks over. The starter — Nub — is ten years old now, fed twice a day with organic stone-ground rye from the Willamette Valley, and it is the reason the crust cracks the way it does.
items: A 20-hour cold ferment so every loaf tastes of wheat, not yeast
Oregon-milled organic flour, delivered whole and sifted in-house
Wood-fired brick deck oven, hand-fed with local oak
Croissants laminated with 84% European-style cultured butter
data.imageCaption: Rosa scoring the day's country loaves, 5:40am

data.menuSubtitle: A short, rotating list of what comes out of the deck oven each morning — pulled from the racks in the order the timers go off.

items.label: Oven lit, six days before you're awake
Loaves shaped by hand each day

data.title: On the ritual of Saturday mornings
data.quote: I have bought a loaf of Ember & Rye's seeded rye almost every Saturday for four years, and I want to say what it's actually like. You come in cold, the windows are fogged from the ovens, and there's that smell — burnt sugar and hot flour and something almost like toast. Rosa knows I take the darkest bake on the rack, the one most people pass over, and she sets one aside without me asking. My daughter learned to say 'kouign-amann' before she could say her own last name. This isn't a bakery I go to. It's the corner my week turns on.
data.personTitle: Bishop's Mill resident, Saturday regular since 2022
data.personContext: Lives three blocks up, walks down in slippers

data.title: From 4am to the front counter
data.subtitle: The unglamorous, flour-dusted hours before the door unlocks — proofing baskets, scored loaves, and the first tray of canelés out of the copper molds.

data.title: Find us on Alder Street
data.subtitle: We open when the first loaves cool and close when the racks are bare — usually mid-afternoon. No reservations, no line-skipping; just come early on weekends.
data.address: 412 Alder Street, Bishop's Mill, Portland, OR 97204
data.hours: Tue–Fri 7am–2pm · Sat–Sun 7:30am–3pm · Closed Mondays (Rosa feeds the starter and rests)

title.value: What comes out of the oven

subtitle.value: The list shifts with the season and the flour we get, but these are the bakes we're known for. Loaves are limited daily and often gone by noon — call ahead if you want a specific one held.

data.menuSubtitle: Breads on the left, viennoiserie and sweets on the right. Everything is baked the morning you buy it; nothing is held over to the next day.

data.title: Order ahead, feed a crowd, or learn to bake
data.subtitle: The retail case is the heart of it, but there are a few ways to get more of Ember & Rye than a single visit allows.

items.title: Loaf standing order
items.description: Reserve a specific loaf — the seeded rye, the 100% whole wheat, the Friday-only walnut-fig — for weekly pickup so it's never sold out before you arrive. Set the day and we bag it with your name on it.

items.title: Whole-cake pre-orders
items.description: Olive-oil citrus cakes, dark chocolate rye tortes, and seasonal galettes made to order with 48 hours' notice for birthdays, dinners, and the occasional apology.

items.title: Wholesale for cafés
items.description: We supply eight neighborhood cafés and two restaurants with morning loaves and croissants, delivered by cargo bike before 7am within a two-mile radius of the mill.

items.title: Saturday sourdough class
items.description: A four-hour hands-on class at the back bench where you build a levain, shape a boule, and take home dough plus a jar of Nub's offspring. Six seats, once a month.

items.title: Coffee & a corner seat
items.description: Eight stools along the window and a single-origin pour-over from a roaster three blocks away, meant for lingering over a warm canelé, not laptops.

items.title: Day-old bread box
items.description: Whatever's left at close, boxed at half price for the last hour — first come, and it disappears fast among the neighborhood regulars.

data.title: The things we won't shortcut
items: Organic flour milled within 90 miles and sifted the morning it's used, never bleached or bromated
A 20-hour cold retard on all sourdough for a blistered crust and a genuinely digestible crumb
Croissants folded through three turns with 84% cultured butter — no margarine, no shortcuts, no par-baked shells
Fired in a single oak-fed brick deck oven; the loaves closest to the coals get the darkest, most-requested bake
Everything sold the day it's made — no freezing, no reheating, no next-day markdowns on full-price bread
Nub, our ten-year-old rye starter, fed twice daily and never replaced with commercial yeast

data.title: Questions we get across the counter
data.subtitle: The practical stuff — timing, holds, allergens, and why we're closed on Mondays.

items.question: What time should I come to get the bread I want?
items.answer: Loaves hit the racks between 7 and 8am and the most popular ones — seeded rye, the country boule, weekend croissants — are usually gone by 11am. If you have your heart set on something specific, call the morning of or set up a standing order.

items.question: Can you hold a loaf or a pastry for me?
items.answer: Yes. Call before 9am and we'll bag it with your name and keep it behind the counter until 1pm. We can't hold indefinitely, so if plans change give us a ring so it goes to someone else.

items.question: Do you take custom cake and pastry orders?
items.answer: We do, with at least 48 hours' notice. That covers olive-oil citrus cakes, chocolate rye tortes, seasonal galettes, and croissant boxes for a crowd. Larger or tiered orders need a week — email hello@emberandrye.com to talk it through.

items.question: Is anything gluten-free or vegan?
items.answer: We're a working flour bakery, so nothing is truly allergen-free and we can't guarantee against cross-contact. That said, several bakes are made without dairy or eggs — look for the vegan mark on the board — and we always have one seeded gluten-conscious loaf on weekends.

items.question: Why are you closed on Mondays?
items.answer: Monday is Rosa's day to deep-clean the oven, refresh the starter cycle, and rest — the same care that goes into the bread. We reopen Tuesday at 7am with a full board.

items.question: Do you deliver or ship bread?
items.answer: We don't ship — good sourdough doesn't survive a courier — but we deliver wholesale to cafés within two miles of the mill by cargo bike before 7am. For individuals, it's counter pickup only.

items.question: Can I use the space to work on a laptop?
items.answer: The eight window stools are meant for a coffee, a warm pastry, and a bit of quiet. We keep it laptop-free during weekend mornings so everyone gets a seat, but weekday afternoons are relaxed and no one will hurry you.

data.title: Never miss the loaf you want again
data.subtitle: Set up a weekly standing order and we'll bag your bread with your name on it before the racks empty.

title.value: The corner of Alder and Ninth

subtitle.value: You'll know it by the fogged windows and the smell before you know it by the sign. Here's everything you need to find us, reach us, or set up a standing loaf order.

data.title: When the door's unlocked
data.subtitle: We open when the first bake cools and close when the racks run bare, which on a good weekend can be well before three. Come early.
data.address: 412 Alder Street, Bishop's Mill, Portland, OR 97204

data.title: Getting here
data.address: 412 Alder Street, Bishop's Mill, Portland, OR 97204

locations.label: Ember & Rye
locations.address: 412 Alder Street, Bishop's Mill, Portland, OR 97204
locations.description: Corner storefront, former print shop, terrazzo floors. Bike racks out front; street parking is easiest before 8am. A four-minute walk from the Ninth Street streetcar stop.

data.title: Start a standing order or ask about a cake
data.description: Tell us which loaf you want held each week, or the date and details of a custom cake or croissant box. We answer between bakes, usually by early afternoon the same day.
data.successMessage: Got it — Rosa or someone dusted in flour will write back this afternoon. If it's urgent, just call the shop at (503) 555-0148.

metadata.structureNotes: Deliberately breaks the generic hero->services->testimonials->cta template: a full-screen atmospheric hero opens into the baker's origin story (aboutImageText) before any selling, a menu block stands in for a services grid to foreground the actual bakes, a single essay-variant featuredQuote replaces a testimonials grid with one regular's love letter, and a morning-bake gallery precedes the visit/contact close.
